text: A buying cooperative is the simplest form of group business — 5-10 entrepreneurs in the same sector pool money to buy supplies at wholesale prices. For example, 5 poultry farmers buying 500 bags of feed together get 20-40% discount compared to buying individually. How to start: find entrepreneurs in the same business, list common supplies needed monthly, identify cheapest wholesale suppliers, pool money and designate one trusted person to buy, distribute supplies fairly with receipts. Benefits include lower costs, shared transport, supplier negotiation power, and quality assurance. The key rule: always keep written records of contributions and distributions. Start with small amounts to build trust before scaling up.

A production cooperative allows 3-5 skilled people to work together on orders too large for one person. This is common in tailoring (school uniform contracts), catering (event services), welding (construction projects), and farming (supplying restaurants). Benefits: handle bigger contracts, share equipment costs, learn from each other, and build a shared brand. How to start: find skilled people in the same trade, register as a group with your Sub-County, create a written MOU covering profit sharing (agree BEFORE you start working), bid for larger contracts together, and share workspace/equipment. The biggest risk is disagreements over money — prevent this by having clear written agreements from day one. Always divide profits based on contribution (who did what work), not equally.

Forming a SACCO (Savings and Credit Cooperative Organization) gives your group access to government programmes like Emyooga (UGX 30M seed capital). You need minimum 30 members with a shared enterprise focus. Steps to register: (1) Hold a formation meeting with at least 30 members, (2) Draft a constitution (template available at MSC offices), (3) Elect leadership — Board of Directors, Supervisory Committee, (4) Register with your District Commercial Office (UGX 50,000), (5) Open a bank account in SACCO name, (6) Complete required financial literacy training, (7) Apply for Emyooga seed capital through MSC. SACCOs can also mobilise member savings, offer small loans, and apply for other government funding. The key to a successful SACCO: strict adherence to constitution rules, regular meetings, transparent financial records, and committed leadership.

A marketing cooperative helps small producers sell their goods at better prices by combining volumes and creating a shared brand. Common in agriculture (joint produce selling), crafts (shared market stall), and food production (branded packaging). For example, 10 honey producers selling under one brand at supermarkets earn 3x more than selling individually at the roadside. How to start: find 5-10 producers of similar quality, agree on quality standards, create simple shared branding (even just matching labels), identify higher-value sales channels (supermarkets, restaurants, online platforms), negotiate as a group for better prices, and distribute earnings based on contribution volume. This model is especially powerful when combined with social media marketing — one Instagram/WhatsApp account for the whole group is much more effective than 10 individual accounts.
